summary: Joel Spolsky announces a new Management Training Program at Fog Creek Software, designed to develop future leaders by rotating them through every non-coding job in the company over three years. He frames the program with an analogy to his experience learning to run a bakery night shift, where mastering every station gave him credibility and holistic understanding. Joel argues against both promoting programmers into management roles they may not want and hiring MBAs who substitute book learning for experience. The program targets smart, ambitious people who aren't necessarily programmers, offering salary, stock, and benefits instead of requiring them to pay for an MBA. He expects the program to produce about twice as many graduates as Fog Creek needs, with the rest going on to start companies or lead elsewhere.
key_points: Management requires different skills from programming, and promoting great programmers into management roles they hate is counterproductive
Holistic experience across every function of a company is the best preparation for leadership, as demonstrated by the bakery analogy
MBAs often substitute academic knowledge for practical experience and common sense, making them less desirable than people with real operational experience
Programmers should have explicit career paths that don't force them into management just to advance in salary or benefits
The program aims to produce more graduates than Fog Creek needs, seeding the industry with well-trained leaders
money_quotes: Many people who are excellent developers are lousy managers, and promoting someone out of a job that they love doing and are good at doing into a job that they hate and are not good at doesn't make sense.

We'd much rather hire someone who created and ran a profitable lemonade stand than someone who has taken two years of finance courses at Harvard, especially since the Harvard MBA is going to think he knows a lot more than he really does.
After a year or so doing every job in the bakery, you have credibility with the crew you're leading because they all know you could do their job.
Nothing can better prepare you for leading a high tech company than gaining significant, substantial experience with everything a high tech company does.
We don't really want to hire MBAs, because there's too much evidence that MBAs substitute book-learnin' for common sense or experience.
We plan to make sure that programmers have explicit career paths that do not require them to shift into management just to get the next raise in salary level or benefits.
Unlike full-time MBA programs, you won't have to pay $120,000 in tuition and $200,000 in foregone salary.
key_insight: The best managers are developed not through academic programs but by rotating through every function of a company, gaining the hands-on experience and credibility that no MBA can substitute.
